title: Levels of organisation — cells to organisms
examTip: You'll be asked to put the levels in order, or give an example at each one. Learn the chain. Know at least two animal examples and one plant example.
introTips: This lesson maps out how those teams are organised — five levels from a single cell to a complete organism.
You'll need to give a specific named example at every level, for both animals and plants.
AQA often asks you to put the levels in order or say what level something belongs to — by the end of this lesson that should be automatic.
intro: Your body has 37 trillion cells. They don't work alone — they're grouped into teams. Small teams form bigger teams. Bigger teams form even bigger ones. That's what this lesson is about.

keyPoints.heading: The five levels — smallest to largest
keyPoints.content: Cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism. That's the chain. Every living thing is built this way. Learn it in that order — you will be asked.
writeBullets: Cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism
This order applies to animals AND plants
Each level is built from the level below it
keyPoints.memTip: Write out the chain five times and test yourself: cover it and say it aloud without looking. Helpful sentence: Cats Take Organs On Order — Cell, Tissue, Organ, Organ system, Organism.

keyPoints.heading: Tissue
keyPoints.content: A tissue is a group of similar cells doing the same job. Muscular tissue contracts. Epithelial tissue lines surfaces. Glandular tissue makes substances. One type of cell, one job — that's a tissue.
writeBullets: Tissue = group of similar cells doing the same job
Epithelial tissue = lines surfaces of organs
Glandular tissue = makes and secretes substances

keyTerms.def: A group of similar cells performing the same function

keyPoints.memTip: Three tissue types to know: Muscular (contracts), Epithelial (lines surfaces), Glandular (makes substances). Acronym: MEG. The heart uses all three — if you're asked to name tissues in an organ, MEG is your go-to.

keyPoints.heading: Organ
keyPoints.content: An organ is made of several different tissues working together. The heart has muscular, glandular and epithelial tissue. So does the stomach. A leaf is a plant organ. More than one tissue type = organ.
writeBullets: Organ = several different tissues working together
Heart contains: muscular tissue, glandular tissue, epithelial tissue
Leaf, root and stem are plant organs
Organs always contain MORE THAN ONE tissue type

keyTerms.def: A group of different tissues working together for a specific function

keyPoints.memTip: The key word is DIFFERENT — an organ has multiple different tissue types. If a question asks what makes something an organ rather than a tissue, that's your answer. The heart is the best example: it has muscular, glandular and epithelial tissue.

keyPoints.heading: Organ system
keyPoints.content: An organ system is a group of organs with the same purpose. The digestive system is your main example — it includes the mouth, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver and pancreas. Plants have organ systems too. Roots, stems and leaves are all plant organs.
writeBullets: Organ system = group of organs with the same purpose
Digestive system: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, pancreas
Circulatory system: heart, blood vessels, blood
Plant organ system: roots, stems, leaves

keyTerms.def: A group of organs working together to carry out a major function

keyPoints.memTip: Learn the digestive system organs in order — the path food travels: Mouth → Oesophagus → Stomach → Small intestine → Large intestine. Add Liver + Pancreas as support organs. Examiners often ask you to name four; having six ready means you'll never be stuck.

tables.title: Levels of organisation — with examples
rows: Group of organs with a shared function

1.question: What is a tissue?
options: A single cell with a specialised function
A group of similar cells working together to do the same job
A group of organs that form a complete system
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — similar cells, same job. That's the definition.
1.wrongFeedback: A tissue is a group of similar cells doing the same job — one step up from individual cells, one step below an organ.

3.question: Which of these is an organ system?
3.correctFeedback: Right — the digestive system is a collection of organs all working together. That's an organ system.
3.wrongFeedback: The stomach is an organ. Muscular tissue is a tissue. A muscle cell is a cell. The digestive system is the organ system — multiple organs, one shared function.

commonMistakes: Calling the heart a tissue — it's an organ. It contains multiple tissue types.
Listing only one tissue type when asked about an organ — organs always contain several.
Forgetting plants — roots, stems and leaves are organs; plants have organ systems too.
Getting the order wrong under pressure — write it out: cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.
examTips: If asked to put levels in order, go smallest to largest: cell, tissue, organ, organ system, organism.
Always give a specific named example — 'muscular tissue' not just 'tissue'; 'digestive system' not just 'a system'.
For the digestive system, be ready to name at least four organs: stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver.

revisionCardBullets: Order (smallest → largest): cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism
Tissue = group of similar cells doing the same job — e.g. muscular tissue, epithelial tissue
Organ = several tissues working together — e.g. heart, stomach, leaf
Organ system = organs with a shared function — e.g. digestive system, circulatory system
The heart contains muscular, glandular and epithelial tissue — it's an organ, not a tissue
Digestive system organs: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, liver, gall bladder, pancreas
Plant organs: leaf, stem, root — plants have organ systems too
